# Transcrição: 04-Articulation

- **Fonte:** 04-Articulation
- **Duração:** 07:14
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:01]

So our final system is articulation. So if you're one of those people who, people are always asking, like, could you repeat that? What did you say? You might be a mumbler, right? And obviously mumbling doesn't project confidence, it doesn't really project that you know what you're talking about. And they've also done some really interesting research in Texas, there was a study, that showed that when people listen to clear speech, well articulated speech, they remembered it better than regular conversational speech. So if you're trying to make an impact with what you're saying, and you want people to remember your message, by amping up your articulation, even just a little bit, you're gonna help them actually retain what you've said. So that's pretty important. Almost all of the articulators, I'll say almost, because our teeth aren't a muscle, but they're technically considered an articulator. But most of them are muscles that we can train. So if you're a mumbler, or if you have a hard time enunciating what you're saying, it just means that you have to train those muscles. We've got to get used to moving our mouth a little bit more, and for most of us, opening our mouth, right? So for me, as a singer, it's really easy for me to open my mouth, because I'm used to doing it for singing. But I find that a lot of speakers, they kind of talk like this, and they're not allowing their mouth to move, and they're also not opening their mouth to allow the sound to come out. So we're gonna do a little face workout. This is to practice moving your mouth, and moving those muscles, the buccinator muscles, the cheek muscles, and the tongue as well. So we're gonna say, this is the smiley face, and then, ooh is our fishy face. Ooh, ee, ooh, ee, ooh, ee. Right? So we're gonna exercise. So say for me, peep-o, peep-o, peep-o, peep. Ready? And... Peep-o, peep-o, peep-o, peep-o. Yeah, and I really want you to exaggerate again. We're gonna go to the extreme so that we strengthen those muscles. Let's try, teet-o, teet-o. Teet-o, teet-o, teet-o. Good. Good. How about... me-mo. Sorry. Me-mo, me-mo, me-mo, me. Good. I see a lot of movement. That's really good. Yeah, and if it helps you to put your hands here to monitor that, that's good. Because I always say in my teaching, muscles don't do words, right? They do feeling. They don't understand words very well. So... Ni-no, ni-no, ni-no, ni. Good. And then let's do ni-nei, ni-nei.

So if it's hard for you, you might have some room to grow in terms of your articulatory strength, flexibility, and speed, right? So one thing that I find that people need to work on is learning to elongate their consonants, because you can get so much meaning and emotion into your speech by elongating your consonants. Now, we're going to practice overdoing it. Again, that's the theme of today. Exaggerate first, learn how to do a sustained consonant, and then you can use it a little bit, and it's going to be really effective in your speech. I really love you. Will you be my best friend? ## [06:02 - 07:14]

Okay? Can you try that first tongue twister? Theothot. Theothot. It's kind of like a spasm, because it's exactly the same theorem. Think about it. Theodosia. Theodosia. Yeah, and it's difficult for us to elongate consonants because we're used to speaking quickly, right? We're not used to saying thick, theorem, but that's how we're going to train those articulators to become stronger, is by elongating them and learning what it feels like to keep your breath going through them. Okay? We're so used to going to the vowel so quickly, and the consonants actually can energize your breath as well, so it's going to make it easier for you to sustain your voice. Okay? The power of the consonants is huge, and it makes you, in singing we always say use the consonants to keep that breath flowing. If your breath has gotten a little bit stagnant, the consonant, and using that energy of the consonant, is going to get it revved up going again. You know what I mean?
